title: Candle Wax on Carpet
timeEstimate: 30 minutes to an hour; fiber check required before any heat
stainChemistry: Candle wax is mostly paraffin (petroleum-derived hydrocarbon) or beeswax/soy wax, often with added fragrance oils and dye. On carpet, wax solidifies into a physical deposit that traps pigment inside — it's more a solid object sitting in the pile than a bonded stain. Two problems: (1) the wax itself is physical, not chemical, and responds to cold (brittle) or heat (melts into absorbent material), and (2) colored wax leaves dye behind that is a separate stain AFTER the wax is gone. The classic internet advice — iron + brown paper bag — works great on cotton but is DANGEROUS on synthetic carpet: nylon, polyester, and especially olefin (polypropylene) can soften, melt, or permanently change texture under a hot iron. Iron-on-carpet advice online is widespread and wrong for most modern carpet.
whyThisWorks: Freezing makes paraffin brittle so the bulk lifts off mechanically — no heat, no risk. What remains goes into a solvent (POG / Goo Gone) that dissolves wax without heat. Dawn emulsifies the residue. Only if fiber is confirmed heat-tolerant (rare for synthetic carpet) is the iron method even considered — and even then only through a heavy barrier. Dye from colored wax is treated as a separate dye stain after the wax is fully gone.
defaultAssumption: Spilled pillar candle / dripped taper / kid's birthday candle on living-room or bedroom carpet. Most home carpet is synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) — do NOT iron it. Wool carpet tolerates careful low heat through a barrier, but wool is also pro-lane for tight stains. When fiber is unknown, default to freeze-and-solvent, skip heat entirely.

spottingProtocol.technique: Identify fiber or default to no-heat path
spottingProtocol.equipment: Good light, carpet tag if available
spottingProtocol.instruction: Identify the carpet: synthetic (nylon, polyester, olefin/polypropylene, triexta) = NO IRON. Wool = iron OK through a heavy barrier with care. Unknown fiber = NO IRON, default to freeze-and-solvent only. Olefin softens under modest heat and will fuse. Polyester can distort. An iron on synthetic carpet can do more damage than the candle wax.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Ice in a plastic bag (never direct ice), plastic card or dull blade
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place ice sealed in a plastic bag directly on the wax. Leave 10–15 minutes until the wax is fully frozen brittle. Never put ice directly on carpet — water damage. Once frozen, crack and lift the wax pieces off with the edge of a plastic card or dull knife. Most of the bulk comes off cleanly this way.

spottingProtocol.agent: POG or Goo Gone — Dissolve Residue
spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent tamp from top with cloth
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or Goo Gone on white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG (professional) or Goo Gone (consumer) to a white cloth. Tamp from the top, flip cloth frequently, work outside-in. Never pour solvent. Ventilate. No flames. 2–3 passes typical for residual wax film.
Work Dawn into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Emulsifies solvent residue and remaining wax. Damp-cloth rinse — never pour.
If the wax was colored and dye remains after wax is gone: 3% H₂O₂ on a damp cloth, 5 min, blot. Hidden-area color test first on dyed carpet. This is now a separate dye stain, not a wax stain.
Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan the area fully dry. Inspect in daylight.

steps: 1. Fiber ID: synthetic = NO iron. Unknown = NO iron. Wool = careful barrier only.
2. Freeze with ice bag 10–15 min. Crack and lift bulk.
3. POG tamp from top, outside-in. Ventilate.
4. Dawn / NSD + soft brush, damp-cloth rinse.
5. 3% H₂O₂ for residual dye (colored wax only).
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold (freeze) + ambient; no iron on synthetic carpet
warnings: NEVER iron on synthetic carpet (nylon / polyester / olefin / triexta) — melts fiber
NEVER iron on unknown fiber — default to freeze + solvent
Iron on wool: low heat, heavy barrier, short passes, constant check
Flammable solvent — ventilate, no flames, no smoking
Never pour solvent on carpet — cloth only
Ice in a bag — never direct ice on carpet

homeSolutions.agent: STOP — Do Not Iron Synthetic Carpet
homeSolutions.instruction: The most common internet advice for candle wax on carpet is iron-plus-brown-paper-bag. That advice works on COTTON fabric. On carpet, it's often WRONG and can permanently ruin the pile. Most modern home carpet is synthetic — nylon, polyester, olefin (polypropylene), or triexta — and every one of those can soften, distort, melt, or fuse under a hot iron. Olefin is the most heat-sensitive. Before you do anything heat-related, figure out what your carpet is. Wool = careful iron through a heavy barrier is possible. Synthetic or unknown = freeze and dissolve, no iron.

homeSolutions.agent: Freeze Method — Safe for All Fibers
homeSolutions.instruction: Get a zip-lock bag, fill it with ice, and press it directly on the wax for 10 to 15 minutes. Never put ice directly on carpet — you'll add water damage to a wax problem. The wax will freeze brittle. Once hard, crack and lift the pieces off with the edge of a plastic card, dull butter knife, or credit card. Most of the bulk comes off cleanly this way. No heat, no fiber risk.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone or POG to Dissolve Residue
homeSolutions.instruction: A thin wax film usually remains after freezing. Apply Goo Gone (orange solvent, any hardware / grocery store) to a clean white cloth — never pour directly on carpet. Press and tamp from the TOP, flipping the cloth as it picks up wax. Work outside-in so you don't spread the edge. 2–3 passes usually clears the film. Open windows. No flames nearby — solvents are flammable.
Work a drop of Dawn in cool water into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Clears the solvent residue and any last bits of wax. Rinse with a damp cloth — never pour water on the carpet.

homeSolutions.agent: If Colored Candle — Treat the Dye
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the wax is gone, you might see a color ring (red wax = red dye, purple candle = purple stain). That's a separate dye stain now. Dab 3% hydrogen peroxide on a white damp cloth, press 5 min, blot. Test on a hidden area first — peroxide can lighten dyed carpet.

homeSolutions.agent: IF Wool Carpet — Iron Method OK With Barriers
homeSolutions.instruction: ONLY if you KNOW the carpet is wool (not synthetic — most modern carpet isn't wool): stack 3–4 layers of white paper towels or a clean brown paper bag on the wax spot. Set an iron to LOW, no steam. Press briefly — 5 seconds at a time — and check. The wax should wick up into the paper. Replace paper as it absorbs. Stop at the first sign of fiber damage. If you're not 100% sure it's wool, don't do this — use the freeze method.

Stack dry paper towels on the spot, weigh down with a book, 30 minutes. Point a fan at the area for 1–2 hours. Inspect in daylight. If residual wax or color remains, one more round of POG/Goo Gone + Dawn can sometimes finish.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Large Spills or Wool
homeSolutions.instruction: For large spills (melted pillar candle), heavily-pigmented wax, wool carpet with a tight spot, or if the freeze-and-solvent protocol doesn't clear the stain: call a pro carpet cleaner. Tell them: "Candle wax on [fiber] carpet, [size], home-treated with ice + Goo Gone/POG + Dawn. [Residual wax / residual dye / didn't attempt iron]. Fiber [known: X / unknown]." Don't let them iron synthetic carpet either.

steps: 1. Fiber ID FIRST. Synthetic or unknown = NO IRON.
2. Freeze with ice-in-bag 10–15 min. Crack and lift bulk.
3. Goo Gone / POG on a cloth, tamp from top, outside-in. Ventilate.
5. 3% H₂O₂ for residual dye (colored wax only; hidden-test first).
6. Wool carpet ONLY: careful iron through heavy paper barrier, 5 sec pulses.
8. Persistent or large = pro carpet cleaner.
products: Goo Gone (or POG if pro-grade available)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if carpet fiber is synthetic/unknown and internet advice is telling you to iron. Stop if iron starts to affect fiber (melt, distort, discolor). Stop if dye ghost persists after 2 rounds of H₂O₂ on a fiber-tested spot.
warnings: NEVER IRON ON SYNTHETIC CARPET — melts fiber
NEVER IRON ON UNKNOWN FIBER — default freeze + solvent
Never direct ice on carpet — water damage
Never pour solvent or water on carpet
Dye treatment is a separate stain AFTER wax is fully gone

neverDo: Never iron on synthetic or unknown fiber
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / triexta: freeze + solvent. NO IRON.
Olefin (polypropylene): freeze + solvent. NO IRON — most heat-sensitive.
Wool: iron OK through heavy barrier, low heat, 5-sec pulses, constant monitor
Unknown fiber: treat as synthetic — no heat

materialWarnings: The viral iron-method is DANGEROUS on synthetic carpet
Dye is a separate stain after wax removal

escalation.when: Wool carpet with tight spot, large melted-candle spill, heavily-pigmented wax with residual dye, or freeze-and-solvent didn't clear the wax.
escalation.whatToTell: "Candle wax on [nylon / polyester / olefin / wool / unknown] carpet, [size], home-treated with ice + Goo Gone/POG + Dawn [+ H₂O₂ on dye]. No iron attempted [/ iron was attempted with X result]. [Residual wax / color ghost / fiber unknown]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with solvent spotting

contextQuestions: What's the carpet fiber — synthetic, wool, unknown?
How big is the wax deposit — drip, stream, pooled?
Was the candle white / unscented / dyed / heavily scented?
Has anyone already tried ironing it?
Is there color visible separate from the wax?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Iron-on-synthetic-carpet is the most dangerous piece of widespread internet advice for this stain. Olefin carpet is especially vulnerable because its melting point is low. Wool carpet tolerates careful heat, but most modern home carpet isn't wool. Default to freeze-and-solvent — it's safe on every fiber.
